01a_quotes.title: Collaborating with AI Agents: A Field Experiment on Teamwork, Productivity, and Performance

study_design: We randomly assigned 2,234 participants to human-human and human-AI teams that produced 11,024 ads for a think tank.
Our study was preregistered and approved by the MIT Committee on the Use of Humans as Experimental Subjects.
Once a participant enters the Pairit platform, they are randomized into a queue for either the human-human or the human-AI condition.
In the human-human condition, the participant collaborates with another human participant. In the human-AI condition, the participant collaborates with an AI agent.
We do not reveal whether or not the partner is a human or an AI until the post-task survey.
To assess real-world advertising outcomes, we ran ad campaigns on the social media platform X, pre-registered on osf.io/95dhu.

Due to the effects of divergent targeting in A-B testing (Braun and Schwartz, 2024), we follow recommendation 5 from Braun et al. (2024) and run a multi-ad study to test the causal effects of the ads on performance, conditional on online algorithms.
task: Teams worked collaboratively to create marketing campaigns for a think tank's year-end annual report, including generating and selecting ad images and writing ad copy and calls to action.
The participants had 40 minutes to submit as many ads as they could produce.
Pairit's left panel is the task panel in which participants can participate in the task work process (in the context of this experiment to create, edit, and submit ads).

The platform includes a carousel of seven pre-selected stock images provided by the think tank that the participants and the AI agent can choose from, and the participants (as well as the AI) can also access the Dall-E 3 application programming interface (API) to generate new images.
The ad copy creation and editing panel includes a headline, primary text, and a description.
population: A total of 2,234 participants, representative of the U.S. population, were recruited through Prolific and randomly assigned to human-human or human-AI teams using the Pairit platform.
We pre-registered a sample of 2,500 participants from Prolific (www.prolific.com) based in the US, with representative stratification across gender and ethnicity.
In total, 2,310 participants completed the task.
Of the 2,500 participants who entered, 23 did not enter the matching queue (see Section 3.3 for details) and were removed from the study.
A further 167 participants quit the study or were timed out before they were matched to a partner.
In 83 human-human sessions (14% of HH teams), the matched partner did not complete the task due to dropout or timeout.

Because these sessions do not represent collaborative work and submissions required confirmation by both participants, we exclude them from all analyses, yielding a final sample of 2,234 participants (1,258 human-AI, 976 human-human).
The study was conducted from October 15 to 18, 2024, and took a median of 46.32 minutes to complete.

To ensure that the randomization procedure successfully balanced covariates across experimental conditions, Table 1 compares key participant characteristics between the human-human and human-AI conditions. These include demographic variables (e.g. age, gender), participants' work status, and the Big Five personality traits.
No significant differences were detected across treatment conditions, indicating balanced randomization across participants.
arms: In the human-human condition, the participant collaborates with another human participant.
In the human-AI condition, the participant collaborates with an AI agent.
In the human-human condition, participants joined a queue until another participant was available, at which point they were paired with each other.
In the human-AI condition, a participant joined a simulated queue, in which they waited for a random amount of time between 1 and 5 seconds, after which they were paired with an AI agent.
The final sample of 2,234 participants (1,258 human-AI, 976 human-human).
outcome_definitions: Productivity is the number of submissions by participants.
Text, Image, Click are human ratings (7-point scale).
Output Diversity is cosine distance to treatment centroid (higher is more diverse).
We use the following as outcome variables: click-through rate (CTR), cost-per-click (CPC), view-through rate (VTR; as a fraction of document viewed), and view-through duration (VTD; in seconds).
For each ad, we asked participants to provide three ratings regarding the quality of the text, the image, and the estimated click through rate. Each item was rated on a 7-point Likert scale.

The first rating was based on the following prompt "The text is present, clear, relevant, and engaging"; the second on "The image is visually appealing"; and the third on "I am likely to click on this ad."
We measure delegation as the fraction of text editing work performed by the partner rather than the focal participant.
We defined delegation as one minus the participant's character contribution divided by the session's total character contribution, which, on average for human-human teams, is mechanically 50%.
To measure output diversity, we embed each submission's ad copy (headline, primary text, and description) using OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small model.
We compute the centroid (mean embedding vector) for each treatment group and calculate the cosine distance from each submission to its own treatment's centroid.
Higher values indicate greater distance from the typical output in that condition, reflecting more diverse or atypical content.
Count is the number of messages sent by a participant.
Task-oriented is the fraction of messages labeled as content or process.
Interpersonal is the fraction of messages labeled as emotional or social.
Copy edits are counts of text changes (i.e., text input events).
statistical_results: Participants in human-AI teams submitted 50% more ads per worker, per unit time than their counterparts in human-human teams, with results consistent when demographic controls are added (Table 2).
In human evaluations of the quality of the ads, we found that human-AI teams produced higher quality ad text but lower quality ad images compared to human-human teams (Table 2).
The estimated likelihood of clicking on the ad was indistinguishable between the two groups.

Using cosine distance to treatment centroids as a measure of output diversity (Table 2), human-AI teams produce outputs that are closer to their condition centroid than human-human teams (β=−0.094 , p<0.001 ), indicating a homogenization of teams outputs.
Our ad campaigns generated 4,932,373 impressions and 7,546 clicks over 20 days.
For CPC, measured in dollars, our analysis reveals no significant effect of collaboration type.
Ads with stronger image quality significantly reduced CPC by $0.29 or 2.5%.
For CTR, expressed as a percentage, there was no direct human-AI effect.
Text quality increased CTR by a small but statistically significant 0.007%.
While there was no direct human-AI effect, text quality was associated with longer viewing duration (p = 0.051).
On average, one point higher text quality ratings were associated with approximately 4% longer view-through duration.
Our analysis of VTR showed no significant effects for collaboration type or quality.
Participants in human-AI teams sent 62% more messages than those in human-human teams, with consistent results when demographic covariates were included (Table 4).
We found that human-AI teams sent 25% more task-oriented messages, while human-human teams sent 18% more interpersonal messages (Table 4).
Participants in human-AI teams made 62% fewer direct copy edits compared to human-human teams (Table 4).
Human-AI teams also delegated 58% of work to AI partners compared to 50% in human-human teams (β=0.084 , p<0.001 ), a 17% increase.
First, participants in the human-AI treatment group were highly accurate in identifying their partner as AI (94.5%), while human-human group participants were relatively less accurate (57.6%).

Participants who correctly identified their partner as an AI agent sent more messages (β3 =11.453 , p<0.001 ), with a higher proportion of task-oriented messages ( β3 =0.061 , p<0.001 ) and a lower proportion of interpersonal messages (β3 =−0.059 , p<0.01 ).

They also delegated more (β3 =0.056 , p<0.05 ) and, despite making far fewer direct copy edits overall in human-AI teams (β1 =−1155 , p<0.001 ), made slightly more edits than participants who did not correctly identify their partner (β3 =150.6 , p<0.05 ).
In human-human teams, task-oriented communication is negatively associated with productivity (β=−4.19 , p<0.001 ), text quality ( β=−0.416 , p<0.05 ), and image quality ( β=−0.267 , p<0.05 ).

However, in human-AI teams, the reverse is true: task-oriented communication improves productivity (β=4.69 , p<0.001 ) and ad quality when working with AI (β=0.746 , p<0.001 for text; β=0.506 , p<0.01 for image; β=0.584 , p<0.001 for click).

In contrast, while interpersonal communication is positively associated with productivity in human-human teams (β=4.25 , p<0.001 ), it is negatively associated with productivity (β=−4.80 , p<0.001 ) and ad quality in human-AI teams ( β=−0.685 , p<0.001 for text; β=−0.581 , p<0.001 for image; β=−0.599 , p<0.001 for click).

In human-AI teams, delegation improves text ratings (β=0.302 , p<0.001 ) and click ratings (β=0.178 , p<0.01 ) but has no effect on image ratings ( β=0.132 , n.s.; Table 6, Panel B), consistent with the jagged frontier of AI capabilities (Dell'Acqua et al., 2023).
Delegation also reduces output diversity ( β=−0.083 , p<0.001 ), explaining the diversity collapse observed in human-AI teams.
Notably, delegation is associated with lower productivity (β=−1.38 , p<0.05 ), perhaps because excessive delegators are less engaged overall.
ambiguity_or_caveats: It is important to note that our study did not include a human-alone condition.

Prior studies have consistently shown that AI tools enhance individual productivity compared to humans working alone across various tasks (Noy and Zhang, 2023; Brynjolfsson et al., 2023; Dell'Acqua et al., 2023; Chen and Chan, 2024; Wiles et al., 2023).
In light of this evidence, our results suggest that the AI in human-AI teams acts as a partial substitute for an additional human collaborator.
Without a human-alone condition, however, we cannot directly quantify AI's incremental contribution beyond what an individual human might have achieved working alone.
We explore this limitation further in the Discussion section.

First, although we identify task-orientation, delegation, and recognition as mechanisms underlying human-AI collaboration dynamics, we cannot fully disentangle whether these patterns stem from the nature of AI communication or from human participants adapting their behavior upon recognizing their partner as AI.

Our mediation analyses tracing the pathway from recognition to communication to delegation to quality are post hoc and correlational; while they are consistent with a causal pathway, alternative explanations remain possible.

For instance, participants who are more comfortable with AI may both recognize it more readily and adopt more efficient collaboration strategies, with no causal relationship between recognition and behavior.
Second, the limitations of current AI models, particularly vision models, present challenges for multimodal tasks.
These models are optimized for next-word generation and specific visual tasks, such as identifying items in images, but are not designed for nuanced assessments like image quality prediction.
This limitation likely contributed to the lower image quality observed in human-AI teams and underscores the need for AI systems tailored to specific creative and evaluative tasks.
However, AI is a moving target and image selection and design competency likely will improve over time.
As with any study of AI, results may drift as AI changes.
Moreover, while our study provides a controlled experimental context, it may not fully capture the complexities of long-term collaboration with AI agents in real-world environments.
Future research should explore how these dynamics evolve over extended periods and in more diverse task domains to validate and expand on our findings.
Finally, our study lacks a human alone condition, which prevents us from directly assessing how human-AI teams compare to individuals working independently.
Without this baseline, we cannot precisely isolate AI's marginal contribution to productivity and the coordination costs associated with collaboration.
Nevertheless, our findings have practical implications for organizations looking to integrate AI agents into collaborative workflows, particularly in tasks requiring creativity and coordination.

Regarding generalizability, our findings should apply to tasks that share key features with ad creation: multi-modal creative tasks requiring both text and visual elements, where AI capabilities vary across modalities (the jagged frontier); iterative production tasks involving multiple rounds of generation, evaluation, and refinement; and settings with objective quality metrics.
Our findings may be less applicable to high-stakes decision tasks where errors are costly and irreversible or tasks requiring deep domain expertise where AI capabilities are more limited.
While our study captured within-session adaptation (humans learning AI capabilities through multiple ad iterations), longitudinal dynamics across repeated collaborations remain unexplored.

The core mechanism we identify (task-oriented communication, higher delegation, and capability-dependent quality effects) should generalize broadly, but the direction of quality effects will depend on the specific task and AI capabilities.
Higher spend consistently lowers costs and suggests divergent targeting and optimization, likely because we had kept on auto-bidding in our campaigns.
Thus, we consider the effects present in this section as conditional on ad algorithms, as per recommendation 5 from Braun et al. (2024).
Interestingly, in our AI evaluations of ad quality, we found that AI ratings were higher on the text and clicks for ads produced by human-AI teams and the same across the groups for image quality.

In a way, it is unsurprising that the AI rated these ads' text quality, image quality, and estimated click likelihood as equal to or better than those produced by human-human teams because these ads were created in collaboration with OpenAI's gpt-4o.

01a_quotes.notes: This is a large-scale randomized controlled trial (RCT) with both lab and field components. The study uses a novel experimental platform called Pairit that enables real-time human-AI collaboration with detailed logging of all interactions. The lab experiment involved 2,234 participants (1,258 in human-AI condition, 976 in human-human condition) creating ads over 40 minutes. The field experiment tested the ads on X (formerly Twitter) with 4.9M impressions. The paper examines three main outcomes: productivity, output quality (text vs. image), and output diversity. Three mechanisms are identified: task-oriented communication, delegation, and AI recognition. The study does not include a human-alone condition, which limits ability to isolate AI's marginal contribution. Results show human-AI teams produced 50% more ads per worker with higher text quality but lower image quality (jagged frontier), and more homogeneous outputs (diversity collapse).

01_extraction.title: Collaborating with AI Agents: A Field Experiment on Teamwork, Productivity, and Performance
01_extraction.study_design_description: A randomized controlled trial in which 2,234 participants were randomly assigned to either human-human or human-AI teams using the Pairit platform. Teams worked collaboratively to create marketing campaigns for a think tank's year-end annual report over 40 minutes, producing 11,024 ads total. The study was preregistered and approved by the MIT Committee on the Use of Humans as Experimental Subjects. Participants were not informed of their partner's identity (human or AI) until after the task. Real-world advertising outcomes were assessed through ad campaigns on X (formerly Twitter) with 4,932,373 impressions and 7,546 clicks over 20 days.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Teams worked collaboratively to create marketing campaigns for a think tank's year-end annual report, including generating and selecting ad images and writing ad copy and calls to action. Participants had 40 minutes to submit as many ads as they could produce. The platform provided a carousel of seven pre-selected stock images and access to the Dall-E 3 API to generate new images. The ad copy creation and editing panel included a headline, primary text, and a description.
01_extraction.population_description: A total of 2,234 participants, representative of the U.S. population, were recruited through Prolific and randomly assigned to human-human or human-AI teams. The study pre-registered a sample of 2,500 participants from Prolific based in the US, with representative stratification across gender and ethnicity. Of the 2,500 participants who entered, 23 did not enter the matching queue and were removed from the study. A further 167 participants quit the study or were timed out before they were matched to a partner. In 83 human-human sessions (14% of HH teams), the matched partner did not complete the task due to dropout or timeout. The final sample of 2,234 participants (1,258 human-AI, 976 human-human) was used for all analyses. The overall attrition rate was 8.9%. The study was conducted from October 15 to 18, 2024, and took a median of 46.32 minutes to complete. Participants were paid $9.

arms.description: In the human-AI condition, the participant collaborates with an AI agent. A participant joined a simulated queue, in which they waited for a random amount of time between 1 and 5 seconds, after which they were paired with an AI agent.

In the human-human condition, the participant collaborates with another human participant. In the human-human condition, participants joined a queue until another participant was available, at which point they were paired with each other.

outcomes.outcome_description: The number of submissions by participants.
Human ratings of ad text quality on a 7-point Likert scale based on the prompt "The text is present, clear, relevant, and engaging".
Human ratings of ad image quality on a 7-point Likert scale based on the prompt "The image is visually appealing".
Human ratings of estimated likelihood of clicking on the ad on a 7-point Likert scale based on the prompt "I am likely to click on this ad."

Cosine distance to treatment centroid. Ad copy (headline, primary text, and description) was embedded using OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small model. The centroid (mean embedding vector) for each treatment group was computed and the cosine distance from each submission to its own treatment's centroid was calculated. Higher values indicate greater distance from the typical output in that condition, reflecting more diverse or atypical content.

The fraction of text editing work performed by the partner rather than the focal participant. Defined as one minus the participant's character contribution divided by the session's total character contribution, which, on average for human-human teams, is mechanically 50%.
The number of messages sent by a participant.
The fraction of messages labeled as content or process.
The fraction of messages labeled as emotional or social.
Counts of text changes (i.e., text input events).
Click-through rate (CTR) expressed as a percentage.
View-through rate (VTR) as a fraction of document viewed.

In human-AI teams, delegation improves text ratings (β=0.302 , p<0.001 ) and click ratings (β=0.178 , p<0.01 ) but has no effect on image ratings ( β=0.132 , n.s.; Table 6, Panel B), consistent with the jagged frontier of AI capabilities (Dell'Acqua et al., 2023).

In human-AI teams, delegation improves text ratings (β=0.302 , p<0.001 ) and click ratings (β=0.178 , p<0.01 ) but has no effect on image ratings ( β=0.132 , n.s.; Table 6, Panel B), consistent with the jagged frontier of AI capabilities (Dell'Acqua et al., 2023).

In human-AI teams, delegation improves text ratings (β=0.302 , p<0.001 ) and click ratings (β=0.178 , p<0.01 ) but has no effect on image ratings ( β=0.132 , n.s.; Table 6, Panel B), consistent with the jagged frontier of AI capabilities (Dell'Acqua et al., 2023).
Delegation also reduces output diversity ( β=−0.083 , p<0.001 ), explaining the diversity collapse observed in human-AI teams.
Notably, delegation is associated with lower productivity (β=−1.38 , p<0.05 ), perhaps because excessive delegators are less engaged overall.

01_extraction.notes: This is a large-scale randomized controlled trial (RCT) with both lab and field components. The study uses a novel experimental platform called Pairit that enables real-time human-AI collaboration with detailed logging of all interactions. The lab experiment involved 2,234 participants (1,258 in human-AI condition, 976 in human-human condition) creating ads over 40 minutes. The field experiment tested the ads on X (formerly Twitter) with 4,932,373 impressions and 7,546 clicks over 20 days. The paper examines three main outcomes: productivity, output quality (text vs. image), and output diversity. Three mechanisms are identified: task-oriented communication, delegation, and AI recognition. The study does not include a human-alone condition, which limits ability to isolate AI's marginal contribution. Results show human-AI teams produced 50% more ads per worker with higher text quality but lower image quality (jagged frontier), and more homogeneous outputs (diversity collapse). The paper notes that AI recognition accuracy was 94.5% in the human-AI condition and 57.6% in the human-human condition. Mediation analyses are post hoc and correlational; alternative explanations remain possible. The limitations of current AI models, particularly vision models, contributed to lower image quality in human-AI teams. Results may drift as AI changes. The study may not fully capture complexities of long-term collaboration in real-world environments. Findings should apply to multi-modal creative tasks with varying AI capabilities across modalities, iterative production tasks, and settings with objective quality metrics, but may be less applicable to high-stakes decision tasks or tasks requiring deep domain expertise. Within-session adaptation was captured, but longitudinal dynamics across repeated collaborations remain unexplored.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly states it is "A randomized controlled trial in which 2,234 participants were randomly assigned to either human-human or human-AI teams." While participants completed a task, this was not a controlled lab setting but rather an online platform (Prolific) where participants worked remotely, making it a standard RCT rather than a lab_task or field_experiment.

per_outcome.operationalization: count of ad submissions produced by participants within the 40-minute session
human ratings on 7-point Likert scale evaluating whether text is present, clear, relevant, and engaging
human ratings on 7-point Likert scale evaluating visual appeal of ad images
human ratings on 7-point Likert scale estimating likelihood of clicking on the ad
cosine distance from each ad's text embedding to the treatment group centroid using OpenAI text-embedding-3-small model; measures diversity/atypicality of content, not speed or quality

fraction of text editing work performed by partner rather than focal participant, calculated as one minus participant's character contribution divided by total; measures work distribution, not output speed or quality
count of messages sent by participant during the session; measures communication activity, not output speed or quality
fraction of messages labeled as content or process; measures communication style, not output speed or quality
fraction of messages labeled as emotional or social; measures communication style, not output speed or quality
count of text input events during ad copy creation; measures editing activity, not final output speed or quality
percentage of ad views that resulted in clicks, measured from actual ad deployment
cost in dollars per click from actual ad deployment; measures economic efficiency, not output speed or quality
fraction of document viewed by users exposed to the ad
duration in seconds that users viewed content after ad exposure

04_onet.rationale: The participants' substantive task was creating marketing campaigns, which centrally involves 'generating and selecting ad images and writing ad copy and calls to action.' This is fundamentally creative work—developing novel advertising content within constraints. While the study measured team collaboration and AI assistance as treatments, the core work activity being performed by participants was creative ideation and content generation for marketing materials. This maps most directly to 'Thinking Creatively' rather than to a specific occupation, as the task is a well-defined creative activity rather than the full scope of a marketing role.

alternates.onet_label: Communicating with Supervisors, Peers, or Subordinates

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm exists (human_ai). This is compared against the human-only baseline (human_human) where participants collaborated with other humans.
llm_extracted.notes: The paper reports a 50% increase in productivity (ads per worker per unit time) for human-AI vs human-human teams. A regression coefficient β=1.954 (SE=0.197) is also reported for the same comparison. The outcome transformation (log, asinh, or levels) is not explicitly stated in the provided data, so regression_outcome_is_log is left null. The measurement unit is 'count of ad submissions' which is a throughput/productivity measure, so larger_is_better. Additional regression coefficients are reported for communication and delegation effects but these are not the primary human-AI vs human-human comparison.
source_quotes: Participants in human-AI teams submitted 50% more ads per worker, per unit time than their counterparts in human-human teams, with results consistent when demographic controls are added (Table 2).

computed.notes: The paper reports a 50% increase in productivity (ads per worker per unit time) for human-AI vs human-human teams. A regression coefficient β=1.954 (SE=0.197) is also reported for the same comparison. The outcome transformation (log, asinh, or levels) is not explicitly stated in the provided data, so regression_outcome_is_log is left null. The measurement unit is 'count of ad submissions' which is a throughput/productivity measure, so larger_is_better. Additional regression coefficients are reported for communication and delegation effects but these are not the primary human-AI vs human-human comparison. | CI missing; variance not computable

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm available (human_ai). Comparing human-AI collaboration (n=1258) vs human-human collaboration (n=976).
llm_extracted.notes: After re-reading the full PDF, the paper still does not report means, SDs, SEs, t-statistics, F-statistics, or standardized effect sizes (Cohen's d or Hedges' g) for the direct comparison between human_ai and human_human teams on text quality. Table 2 (page 22) shows only the regression coefficient β = 0.327 (SE 0.024) with p < 0.001 (inequality only). Table G (page 51) provides the full regression model with covariates but still only reports coefficients, not raw means or SDs. The paper uses regression-based analysis throughout rather than reporting descriptive statistics by arm. Without means+SDs, t-statistics, F-statistics, or reported effect sizes, Hedges' g cannot be computed. The p-value is reported only as an inequality (p < 0.001), not an exact value. Sample sizes are confirmed from Table 1 (page 16): n_human=976, n_ai=1258.
source_quotes: Table 2: Collaborating with AI agents improves productivity and text quality but lowers image quality (i.e., a jagged frontier), and decreases output diversity. Human-AI: β = 0.327*** (SE 0.024) for Text quality
Notes: *p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001. Productivity is the number of submissions by participants. Text, Image, Click are human ratings (7-point scale) with standard errors clustered at the ad level.
